author: timbeiko
content: TL:DR on “why burn?”: we don’t have mechanisms to agree to how to split funds. A burn solves this problem because it makes every existing holder’s ETH a proportionally larger share of the current supply, so they are free to allocate those funds as they please.

author: timbeiko
content: For example, if you own 100 ETH and the supply is 100M ETH, you own 0.0001% of the supply. If we burn 50M ETH, you now own 0.0002%, and you could decide to spend 50ETH to get back to owning 0.0001%. These are example numbers and don’t take into account new issuance of ETH/fiat price fluctuations, obviously.

author: luigylemon
content: We will never have those mechanisms if there’s no common will. Individuals can’t solve problems that apparently even countries can’t coordinate between themselves.

author: shamuzzlik
content: The coins are burned, but the value is still there.

author: luigylemon
content: I will use the forums to explain in more length why I don’t think that’s true. You guys suggest research or the magicians one?

author: timbeiko
content: I think the rough consensus is people don’t want those mechanisms *in the protocol*, and instead want this at the application level.

author: __climber__
content: Is this in reference to the opposition towards EIP-2025?

author: timbeiko
content: Yes, and just my broader read of the community + having had these conversations about 1559 as we were planning it 😅
